speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Grace Wicks relates many memories of early day life around Genesee, including the great importance of good housekeeping, heart and hand marriages, the stock show celebration, Genesee stores and cultural events, playing piano at the local movie house and the big city of Lewiston. She describes the trying World War I years in her family with the terrible injury of her brother Lou, and also the major family undertaking that was Wash Day.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: I'd like to talk about Wash Days I can remember. I can remember I'm at my grandparents home, where my great auntie was struggling to wash the long woolen drawers of my grandfather, her brother, her younger brother. Her name was Mary Jane Lanphear and his was Joshua Giles Lanphear and they lived in Troy, Idaho at this time. He had problems with his elimination and this meant that she had a terrific task of trying to keep him tidy. He wore the heaviest of woolen drawers and long shirt with long sleeves and high neck, winter and summer. He said in the summer it insulated him and I guess it did because he never seemed to be over warm. In the winter he needed it, so that's the way he dressed year in and year out. Over the washboard those heavy garments would become almost as rigid as boards. But she would struggle away with the yellow soap and the washboard and eventually out would come a very clean garment, which was dense hung on the line so that it got all the sweetness of outdoor air. And then they were ready for the next time. These people were as clean as any in their time, but they bathed once a week and changed their underclothing once a week. And grandfathers slept in his underwear. They also had heavy blankets on the bed and I can remember one of the most uncomfortable nights of my entire life in their home. When I was put to sleep in the guest room between mother and dad, which was a warm position anyway, and up and down my back was this hot prickly feeling that robbed me of my rest. It was caused by hand -woven woolen sheets, not blankets, sheets. You could see the warp and woof very plainly in their texture and they were a prize possession set aside for the delight of company in winter weather. Being, as I found many years later, completely allergic to wool, I broke out in red welts all up and down my back and thought that was a very, very uncomfortable situation. I thought Troy was a great metropolis, however. The population was probably 375. Because there was a train that went through at night on the Northern Pacific track and their cows who grazed around the village had bells. Now that was the end of sophistication to me. So to wake in the night or perhaps during this miserable night, I can remember the satisfactions of hearing those two sounds to which I was strange and which meant something matching to me. Now another wash day I remember very clearly was at home on the farm. We had a washer which was a wooden tub with a handle on it on the side and it turned back and forth with some vigorous arm propelling it. 400 times for the dirty towels, 400 times for the dirty men's shirts, much less time for the tea towels, and much less time for the ladies undergarments. Of course mother didn't stop with one petticoat. She had one inner petticoat and two outer petticoats and this was for every day. When she dressed up she had more. She had a corset cover. She had all of the proper fixings to be adequately covered at all times. This made for lots of washing and in due time my older sister joined her in a number of garments and so there was lots of light underwear. The towels, in addition to those 400 turns, were put to boil in heavy lye suds on the big old kitchen range in an enormous washer boiler. This had a lid and in due time it would just boil and bubble and smell up the house and make steam in the house and that was a delicious odor of cleanliness. Often times dad would help us, many times the big boys, but sometimes it would be dad and when it was I was always glad because he was a generous treater. Mother didn't believe in in between meal snacks but father was a little more lenient with small hungry people and from time to time he would slice off some of her delicious homemade white bread. Often times dark bread but more often white and put on a thick covering of good homemade well -worked butter and then for the pure ambrosia he would put on plenty of sugar. Now this was a wash day treat because dad wasn't in the house any other time but I remember it to this day and walking out into the yard feeling simply on cloud nine and listening for bluebirds or watching any of the exciting events that the animals created round about and thinking that this was a very very nice world. Now the next wash day that I remember came about in a different way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mother had electricity in town after 1916 which means I was ten years old and she wanted an electric washer but how to get it?

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Father's income, he told me one time, was such that sometimes he was glad for even an extra calf at the end of the year. So it wasn't any use to look to dad to buy the electric washer but she had follow me and there was Ruby that hot -blooded riding horse and one way and another she just figured out that if she sold me a horse, she could sell those two very fine possessions, she could maybe get some things that would do her more good. So she sold follow me who went for a very good price and broke all our hearts but this was par for the course when an animal was part of one's business and later sold Ruby. And this yielded enough money for an electric washer, an excellent Maytag with an agitator middle and a round roll ringer and it also yielded enough money for a cable Nelson piano.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, was this a real goal?

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Not for anyone but me because the rest of the family had to listen to me thump. But for years my mother had been having me go to her friends to take music lessons on a piano and then come at 1130 each morning from school and stop by for 30 minutes good practice before I came home for lunch. Which of course at our house at noon was dinner. So I was pretty well along in taking lessons on how to play the piano and here I had one of my own. For the first three days I thought it was disloyal not to play it and I'm sure the family must have nearly lost their minds but they were very indulgent and very loyal and they never once indicated that they were completely tired of that banging. I had a number of nice little pieces and both father and mother showed me off to every guest that came and this was great stuff when you were at that age. Now the wash days I remember with the Maytag were also unique in one way. Oh, let me retrogress and go back to the farm because after all of these turns on the washer and all of the ringing that some sturdy arm had to do because mother's was not equal to it. And all of the boiling and then all of the rinsing and mother was a great rinser because she felt that soap left in garments was not good for people's skin let alone her precious family's skin. So she was very careful to rinse and of course everything was spring water and the least bubble of soap showed so she would put it through one or two or three waters. Then it would be rung the final time and put upon the clothes lines. Father was never one to put up a clothes post. He depended on nature or what was handy. For example, a droopy wire could go from the corner of the toilet to the corner of the bedroom of the house or it could go from the pear tree over to the corner of the porch or wherever there would be space indicated. And that's where we'd hang our clothes along toward four in the afternoon having begun good and brisk and early in the morning. And those clothes would stay out all night and we hoped it wouldn't drain and wouldn't blow them. But if it did we had very sturdy pins and very sturdy precautions taken so that they wouldn't blow off. Many times they wrapped around and got very tangled, especially the legs of the men's long pants. But otherwise the clothes just stayed there until they finally dried in good weather that is. In wintertime, oh what a struggle to get the clothes dry. And if you were so in such a position as to have to wash the winter cotton blankets, to get them dry meant having draperies of wet steaming garments around the living room for days with the heater going full blast. Oh usually I'm exaggerating on that, usually a day and a night did it. But most of us just suffered it through and kept us clean as possible. Then the clothes had to be brought in and the kitchen table would be heaped high and it was a big kitchen table that seated ten. And it would be stacked with these stiffly frozen sometimes clothes or stiffly dried clothes and then would come the task of folding them and putting them away. But most of the time when they would have been hung outdoors we were rewarded by their delicious smell and how nice we knew they were going to be to use. And so we didn't mind much. But the folding process was yet another one of the tasks of laundry. A big one, matching all the socks for seven, eight or so people. Doing all of the towels in the stack and all of the tea towels in the stack and all of the underwear for various people in their stacks. Oh my, this took a lot of sorting and a lot of doing. To go back to the electric washer during World War I when father came back to take care of growing food and the boys were in the service. We had to go to town to use the electric washer and this meant a full day. Mother would pack up all of the dirty clothes and me and into the buggy we'd go and into town we'd go with whatever other errands she had to do. And we'd get down to the house and open up the closed smell of it and get water heated. We had a faucet for cold water and we had a reservoir for heating water. But coming in in the summer meant that we had to fire up the old range and get it going and the sweat would pour and the wood would have to be gathered and the whole bit but we'd get things going and in due time start the washer and then get them out on the line as fast as ever we could. So that we could go home that night with a dry batch of clothes. This took a lot of doing and there wasn't very much time for a little girl to get to that piano which she'd been deprived of for a few weeks or a few days or whatever it was. And my goodness I didn't go to the lengths of hunting up a little girlfriend to play with, there just wasn't time. If I had a minute I dropped into the piano and had my fun. That was a real pleasure. And in World War I we had a number of catchy songs like Over There and Keep the Home Fires Burning and It's a Long Long Trail and then the other one It's a Long Long Trail to Tipperary. All of these tunes that had a great spirit and then there was the Nostalgic Love Song of Till We Meet Again and Marquita and Delightful Tunes which I thoroughly enjoyed playing and singing and oftentimes mother sang with me. We'd have a little fun along with artwork. Now I am torn between two ways of washing. Our apartment house affords yet an agitator washer with an electric roll ringer. Using this is a real privilege because one keeps one's clothes so beautifully white and beautifully bright colored because you never mix colors in that kind of washer, you just use another washing, another tub full of water. Now in an automatic washer which is downstairs and which we can use for 25 cents, I frequently go down but it's for specialized occasions because if I yield to the temptation of putting a full load in and load in dark colored things with my light colored underwear, pretty soon I have a slightly dingy look to the whole light batch of clothing that I want to wear or use and this just makes me cringe. So oftentimes I don't use the quick way of washing for the 25 cents. I go upstairs and take the time that it takes to do a good job and that's just what I finished now. Now some of the other things I wanted to talk about this morning, we were talking about the boys going into World War I. This meant that my father who was then along toward 50 or in his 50s with a bad back had to take the six horse team of costly mules that the boys could handle with their young vigorous shoulders and use them to plow and do the other farm of harrow and do seed, do the other farm chores. Those animals were Missouri mules and they cost $800 a span which was outrageous when you could get a good team of horses for 250. But these mules were marvelous animals except that they had hard mouths. By a hard mouth I mean that they had been abused along the way and there were calluses on their lips etc. so that they didn't respond readily to being pulled in guidance by a bit. So dad's poor old shoulders would get so tired and his back nearly break across the small of it and he'd come in and sit on the kitchen stove oven door so that the heat would pour out upon his tender muscles and hope for some relief before he'd go back and try to do his work again. Of course he would have had it done by someone if he possibly could but there was no available man to be found. My next older brother than I was four years older and he just was too young to take on the task. My oldest brother startled my folks by coming in one day and saying I'm enlisting. Well of course no parent likes to hear that but I remember dad setting his mouth and the tears coming to his eyes and he's swallowing right back and sticking out his hand to Lou and saying I'm proud of you. And so Lou went to enlist in World War I. This was 1917 but he was rejected. He was rejected because he only had one fifth of vision in his left eye. What had happened was that as a boy he had been in the barn tending the animals and there was a wire stretched from one end of the barn to the other and on that a lantern hung and could be moved from place to place to light whose ever task was at hand. One time that wire broke and by some ill chance the broken end landed square in Lou's left eye and took four fifths of his vision. It was the left eye so he felt that he perhaps would pass the physical examination and it was only then that we realized how little vision he had had all this time in his left eye. So he was rejected but in due time his number came up in the draft. He went to be examined and was taken. He landed eventually in a machine gun battalion which was the crack shot battalion in the AEF in France once the war was over. He could shoot with that right eye like a million dollars and it didn't bother one bit that his left eye was nearly blind. Ben came along to be also drafted and he went into a remount section. This meant that as his life had always been with animals so it continued to be and he worked horses and mules through the entire war and saw no action. However Lou's service was so filled with lack action that he still is suffering from it. He was such a young and hardy man but a dreadful thing happened to him in the battle of shadow theory. He was a big shell burst right in front of him and knocked him backward into a trench. He landed on the back of his neck which didn't break his neck but which so damaged it that for many years he had the most excruciating pain from time to time that would last usually about three days. His face would be livid and his eyes washed of color and he would be so very ill during that time and the doctors at the vet's hospital told him that until his neck became rigid he would have to endure this pain. But they could give him no compensation because when this happened to him he was gathered up and taken to hospital and no connection of record was made of his problem though he was eight months in hospital. During this time he was given drugs to ease his agony and until he was too dependent upon them then he was broken of that dependence and all of this took much time and yet never were the records cleared that this was a soldier hurt in action. Finally after twenty years or so he received eleven dollars a month which was better than nothing but which always seemed great irony to the family. I visited him recently now that he's eighty years old and in the nursing home in Lewiston and he barely can move his legs. He said this was all stemmed from when he had a forced march along with his battalion into Germany through the mud for twenty four solid hours and there wasn't one drop of water or one bite of food for them during the day. And when they got to their destination there was food there and many soldiers became violently ill when they ate but Lew said he escaped because first he drank a great deal of water so he was able to stand it. It's often that the first class private has caustic comments to make on the leadership of the second lieutenant who is in charge of him and his buddies. Lew's comment along this direction was of time when he was standing guard and everybody else was asleep in an exhausted sleep because they had been in an offensive. And they'd had no food for such a long time and it came and the lieutenant bounded up and he said wake everybody up here's food. And Lew went over and took the lid off the can of stew and out to him came the odor of the sourest most spoiled batch of food you ever smelled. And he told the lieutenant and the lieutenant was a little dingy and he said oh wake him up wake him up and Lew said what's the reason what's the good of it. They're so tired and they can't eat this. Well but those are orders wake it up well wake him up. Lew said I'll shoot you before I'll do it and he said you know that young man was younger than I was and he just kind of turned around walked away and he never did give me a bad time about that but he said I'd have killed him.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: They got pretty short tempered I'm sure when they were that tired and had been on that kind of wretched business that long.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: When letters would come from my big brothers I would be bringing the mail home to my folks. I would stop on route from school at the mail at the post office and our mailbox was to 21 and the combination was one on J and one on R. Now a pioneer merchant in Genesee was Jacob Rosenstein so we always said the combination was Jacob Rosenstein one on J and one on R. And if there was a letter from the boys and oh my those were infrequent but if one did get into that mailbox I'd go home ten feet tall stepping high barely able to get there. So that mother could sit the envelope and we all could read how the boys were and we'd look at that postmark so hard because at least we knew they'd been alive to write the letter. But news of hurts and news of their doings was all too infrequent during the time that Lou was in this hospital and unidentified as it were. He received not one letter from home or one scrap of mail because he was just lost and he received not one cent of pay during that whole long eight months. This puts sand in the craw of a human being and Lou has always been quite a quiet taciturn over serious man but I'm sure a personality that couldn't measure up to that kind of adjustment would never have survived in wholesomeness mentally through such an ordeal.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: There are many hurts in war that can't be counted on the casualty list and nobody hurts hates war more than I. And as each one has engulfed my life this one first and my husband in World War two my brother in law and nephews in the Korean War and my son in the Vietnamese War each time I have to make my terrific adjustment.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Now we were going to talk about some other things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What Sam today? Oh I was going to talk about heart and hand marriages weren't I?

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Well heart and hand marriages had to be more or less resorted to in pioneer times because as I've said before in these interviews so many more men came on the adventuresome trail of conquering the west than women did. And so the men had to do the best they could and if they were respectable people they wanted to marry the women that they consorted with and they wanted to establish homes and raise families. And so they would send back maybe for somebody they remembered or maybe they'd go home and marry a widow of an old friend or maybe they themselves were widowers and they'd go back and marry a younger sister or a younger person in the town from which they'd come. One couple that I know knew very well were wonderful people. The man's name was Will Nixon. He was a highly respected farmer in Genesee and a very close friend of my family. In fact at my father's funeral he said to me, Grace I think I am your father's oldest friend who will be here. But a man named Joe Hasford stepped up and said no Will I think I'm his oldest friend here. And so they had a little exchange both of them being well along in their eighties. Dad was seventy three. And Mr. Nixon and mother called him Will and dad called him Will but to us children he was always Mr. Nixon. And he needed his wife and so he sent back east and I don't remember just how but it had something to do with letter writing so this wasn't true heart and hand but it was a lettered courtship. And he asked this lady, he asked the lady whose name was Mae but I don't know her maiden name who was a very refined and lovely looking person who wore glasses with a delightful little chain that went back to an earpiece and which had a very feminine looking nose picture across the bridge of her pretty nose. She was a lovely person and it was their oldest child that made the spot in our raspberry patch but they lost this boy early in life and then they had another daughter Francis who was a lovely girl but she died in her teens also from a quick throat infection. And that left only the girl my age also named Grace. She presently is a retired school teacher in Clarkston and is the heir to the vast family holdings which were choice on the south side of Genesee and on down the central grade well laid out land which of course has no heir. Mr. Nixon was a scholarly man as was his wife. They both were students and very intelligent people but when Mrs. Nixon came to the depressed years of the menopause her personality became quite ingrown and one day she walked into the Snake River.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Another couple that was a true heart and hand couple were the man was very handsome but the woman was definitely plain and they both had signed up with this agency which would get lonely people together. She had come west and had married this gentleman who had a fine farm south of Genesee where the family still in third fourth third generation is farming and they quarreled. They were known to be a quarrelsome couple. They tried to keep their family squabbles private but their children were also contentious in some instances. They had three. It was an unpleasant domestic situation always but they lived out their lives together of over 40 years just with pure endurance. They had one daughter who was happy and pretty and she got away from home early a beautiful woman. They had a son who was by streaks exceedingly charming and exceedingly exasperating. He went to the University of Washington and was quite a hell raiser but he married a lovely girl from Troy and they had a nice family. The son of which is presently farming the home place. There was also another couple. The woman, well there were three of them, the women were half breed Indians. In each case the people lived out their entire lives together, produced very handsome children who adjusted well to society and who were not ashamed of their antecedents, who were glad they inherited Indian land and who lived very useful and pleasant lives well accepted by everyone. As a matter of fact I know a fourth one. One of these couples where the half breed part came in, the man was an army man who retired straight as a ramrod in his saddle and his full blooded wife was a dear woman and she was shaped just about like an egg. Fat, a little greasy, black eyed, lank -haired and dull of expression but she was a fine woman just the same and produced two very nice daughters. I remember her one time coming to my mother's home at mother's invitation and unwrapping a beautiful white elk Indian dress, beaded and trimmed with porcupine quills and elk teeth and fringe. It was a work of art. I don't know where it is presently but I'm sure that it is not destroyed and if we can get it for the museum here it would be a real treasure. I don't suppose anyone has approached the family to having it preserved in such fashion and it might be something we could pursue because the half breed daughter still lives in a lovely retirement home over on the coast. She had the start of her Indian grant and her husband was a good farmer and they also acquired what the old soldier had accumulated so that they had a very fine start. And they went on from there and they are comfortable indeed. I wouldn't say wealthy but certainly comfortable. As a matter of fact there wasn't a single one of these couples which had the start from the Indian land but what did very well. There was one Indian woman whom I haven't told you about. Her name was Lily Viles and she had a teepee in her door yard and part of the time when she wanted to in the hot summer she slept out there. Now the silly story and I'm sure it was a silly story these days but as a child I believed it was that she had human scalps along the border of her room but I think this is completely erroneous. It just made a good story. Her daughter was married to a white man and lived over just a little way and I could see Mrs. Viles home stay her Indian land and the teepee when I rode up to the gate for a little ride.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: What else were we going to talk about?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We were going to talk about a thousand dollar Annie.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Oh yes many women were great helps to their husbands in those days where everybody was scrambling as best they could to better their lives and their standard of living. This woman was an immigrant from Scandinavia. I don't know which of the three or four countries but she stopped on route west to work as a maid in New York City. These immigrant girls were highly prized as domestic help. They were strong, they were willing, they were clean, they were in many instances skilled. Well she had a thousand dollars saved up when she came west and married this gentleman. Their standard of living was pretty sub for a number of years and I suppose they were resorting to peasant standards in order to get a start because they certainly did well. The stories were told that when the boys got out of bed in the morning the loaf of bread, the loaves of bread were put in the warmth of the blankets so that they would rise. Now that's just a real good story but whether that happened or not is anybody's guess and I rather imagine it didn't but then it could have been the practical way. One time after this family had two big half grown boys and two younger daughters, they stopped on route to a 4th of July celebration down at Spalding at my grandfather's place and bought an entire tree's fruit of sour cherries. They picked it hastily and stripped that tree in very cleanly fashion. By cleanly I mean they picked all of the fruit and loaded it into the wagon where the cook stoves sat. And down the hill they went to celebrate the 4th but their idea of celebrating the 4th was a very pragmatic one. They got the fire going in the cook stove and the two boys just got on their hands and knees and some boards were laid across them. And Mrs. ABC began rolling out pie dough as fast as ever she could and she baked cherry pies. She didn't take time to take the seeds out but she baked cherry pies which sold for a whole great big dollar apiece. Now that was a full day's wages so if we were paying 20, 30, 40 dollars for a cherry pie these days we'd understand what good sports those men were to buy those pies at a dollar apiece. Well they baked pies until the cherries were gone and had a very profitable day and how much the kids got to celebrate was problematic and certainly beside the point so far as the parents were concerned. Another story from this family is one which I find quite touching. They grew pigs and it was wise to use stubble pasture for pigs because there'd be lots of dropped grain and seeds that had good nourishment in the windrows of the crop. And so the pigs would be pastured, herded by some person and oftentimes they were taken over to the Craigmont area which we called the Camas Prairie. There are several Camas prairies in Idaho so this is the one that was near the town of Craigmont. And there the human being who was the pig herder would camp and have a saddle horse and a very simple arrangement for living but would keep those pigs where they were supposed to be and they would thrive on the food that they could forage. When the weather got cold toward fall then the herder was obliged to herd them back over here to home. Well now the herding chores fell to the lot of the young teenage daughter, a very refined and nice girl in every way who married well and was exceedingly respected in the community. But when she stopped her herd to rest for the night and it took more than one day to bring the stock across the long distance, she had to bed down just where the herd was. And one time my sister was shocked to find this nice girl asleep beside the side of the road as dad and my sister rode by in the buggy and there was this girl resting in her bed right alongside the dusty road. But this was pioneer necessity and it meant that the girl had lots of gimp to be able to do that. She went on to inherit very nicely from her parents and her husband inherited from her because she died young with tuberculosis.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: The vast holdings of that family, one way or another, is still held, still enjoyed and still very well handled. They are good people. This country was settled by a fine class of people, not always so many social graces, but so far as character was concerned they merited the respect of each other. And while they didn't always have the same standard of living, these people helped each other and were kind to each other, lent money to each other and built a world. Regarding what they did for each other, let me tell a story of my grandmother and her sugar bowl of Oregon grape jelly. Sugar was at a premium. It cost 19 cents a pound and it was black as it could be, almost just solidified black strap molasses, but at least it was sweet. And grandmother brought, when she first came here from Oregon, a sugar bowl full of Oregon grape jelly, of great luxury. And like all neighbor women, she was called in to help at the confinement of a neighbor. And she went over to her, and I think I can tell the name of this. My grandmother was Adelia Jane Mrs. Lewis, and the woman to whom she was called was Mrs. Wall, W -A -H -L, the first in the area of that name. And Mrs. Wall was being confined with twins, and grandmother helped to bring them into the world. And as a great treat, took along the bowl of jelly, and teaspoon by teaspoon this was put into cool spring water and stirred up for a little cordial as a lift for the person who was ill. This made many the bedsides of many sick people. There was never pay for this kind of kindness. This was a neighbor's love for a neighbor. Speaking of lending money,

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Lola Clyde told me this story, and perhaps she can tell it better. But she told of a member of the Wall family going to borrow money from a person who was in the area, but wasn't a close neighbor, and so of course had no friendship involved, especially. And the money was in a kerosene oil can in the corn crib, pretty good early day bank. And the money was taken out and lent to Mr. Wall, and 18 % interest compounded monthly was charged. These were before the days of usury, and the renting of money then was very costly. Of course now in car contracts when we pay 38 % interest, we don't blink an eye. But that 18 % of those days looked pretty bad in every way, but only had to have the money. And that gentleman told my dad that he would never get out of debt, and his son would never get out of debt, and his son's son would never get out of debt. But I lived to see the day when not only were all three out of debt, thanks to the bounty of this wonderful country here, but from the acres that were preserved in the family's holding, all of them were comfortable indeed, and still are, and so are their descendants. This is a magnificent legacy, and to me represents the deep worth of America, that can do this for people who were born overseas and came here. My grandfather was such just that close our way to the European soil. And I think that when you ponder this at all, you are deeply grateful for the privilege it has been to live the lives of hard work, yes, but great pleasantness and comfort we have known in this area, where a sense of place is so important, where we feel such deep roots, where the house we lived in, one of which still stands, where in one corner our dear little aunt Carrie died at age 15 of spinal meningitis, and where in another corner the only baby of Adelia and Louis that was born in Idaho was born. This is, I think he was born about 18, now they came in 78, and he was born about 1880 to 82, because he was about 10 years old when mother was married, and she could hold out her arm and he could walk right under her arm. So he was just a little boy when his older brother married my mother.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Now what else were we going to talk about?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We have to discuss about the way that people needed to, are always ready for people being in the house, and the fact that you always had to be dressed well.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Oh well, goodness sakes, if you had any self respect, you didn't go around looking like a mess, because anyone stopping by would judge the condition of your house and the condition of your person, along the scale of cleanliness and readiness and personality and the whole bit, by how you appeared at the door, and you certainly didn't go to the door in any bathrobe or dressing gown or anything but proper clothing. Hired men were around and they were strange men in your home. Maybe the school teacher was there, maybe guests were there. So when you got out of bed in the morning, you washed your face and combed your hair and put on clean clothes. And then you got breakfast, and you didn't have an ugly looking table, you had a cleanly table with your food as attractively set out as you could. It was not only important that it be abundant and delicious, but that it look well. And your kitchen floor was swept and it was mopped and it was clean. And when somebody went to your cupboard and opened the door, they didn't find the mess. You were your own servant, so when you created food and set it on the table, they wanted to be sure it was clean. When you invited somebody to stay all night at your house, you wanted to be sure that there was ample providing for cleanly pleasant beds. This took a lot of work, but goodness, extra stuff like putting on a clean apron when you went out into the yard to talk to somebody. You certainly didn't go around with your tummy wet from doing the washing or anything else. People didn't take each other casually. People took each other at face value and you saw to it that was your best foot forward if it could be achieved. And to any doing woman, it can be achieved if she's taught that way. And if a man is taught that way, he doesn't go around sloppy either. And when you go into their houses, there isn't an ugly odor of unaired human beings or sleep or over old cooking smells. You have charm in not only the cleanliness and welcoming smells, but orderliness so that life is pleasant and comfortable. And it wasn't that women were trying to be snobby by being good housekeepers or that their men were overly conscious of this effort women made. It was part of their pride in homemaking. You didn't do a lousy job. You had as nice looking things around as you could. You had them in order. You had things ready for your family to wear when they needed them. If you sat up two -thirds of the night making a graduation dress, your daughter went across that stage in as lovely fashion as you could bring about. This was your love and your pride in your home. And for people to be scruffy to that class of women was just lazy. To not be provident for the table by having delicious canned fruit, or cookies baked ahead, or your bread which was fine -grained, or your butter which was properly worked so there were no white streaks from too little preparation. Or whatever it was you served, you did it as well as your ability and your time and skills would allow. It was part of self -respect. And that's why people have been so antagonistic to hippies. Because the older people see in that a lazy, scruffy attitude toward life. It just wasn't their way. Now of course many times, not many times, but sometimes we've heard the story about the woman who wanted to be the first one to have the whitest and earliest wash on the line. So she just went to the drawer and took out the clean pillowcases, ran them through the water and went outside to hang them up. She beat her neighbors. Well of course there were silly little stories like that, you know, regarding good housekeeping. But I tell you, to live where women do all their own work, as all of us did, and where we had very few helps from the grocery store, it meant planning, it meant devoted attention. And also the nickel had to be stretched at all points. So it meant thrift. And when I would come home from school and enter my mother's sweet -smelling, home with food cooking and the table ready and the food all just waiting to be dished up, that's the way we called it. It was to be dished up and put on the table. Well I tell you now that was a joy that just went to your very heart. And that's why it was done that way. It was to make people happy.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: And it isn't a bad way to live, is it?

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: No, it wasn't a bad way to live. My mother's ingenuity has paved so many of the rough spots in her life. For example one time, down on the ranch, there were no preparations for Christmas because it was such a lean year financially. So she sent Dad down to the canyon where there were no conifers growing at that time. And he got a branch from one that he finally found and brought it up and they leaned it against the wall carefully and banked around its stem. And she had some orange crepe paper. Wonderful, wonderful. And she filled it with rags in little round balls and made decorations for the pine branch and hung the little orange balls on it. And she took one of Dad's empty cigar boxes and covered it with some material she had from somebody's outgrown something or other and made a beautiful little sewing box. And in that she put a thimble and some needles and some little rolls of threads adroitly placed around matching paper so that my little sister had a sewing box for Christmas. And that was her Christmas. And Dad made a home -waved sled, which was the two big boys present together. I wasn't born yet. And I don't remember what she fashioned for the baby, but undoubtedly something. And so they had Christmas after all. And that was just how they managed. Another time I remember when Dad and Mother put our stockings on the backs of chairs and just lightly attached them there. I don't remember how they made them stay. But this was a nice Christmas because I got a doll. And in the tow of Mother's stocking there was a five dollar gold piece from her husband. And in his a gold watch fob from her. Also sticking out of the stocking for Dad was a piece of coal because he'd been so naughty. Of course he got great pleasure out of having a little joke. He was always getting fun out of something. I remember his fiftieth birthday. We were all invited to his Mother's for dinner. And to Grandmother Jane, the nicest dinner you could have were crisp, creamy biscuits with good, boiled hen made into rich gravy and the pieces of chicken all slathered through it. And they'd just put all over these biscuits. I don't know whether we had that day or not, but I'll bet we did. And so Dad's brothers spread -agled him in the archway between the dining room and parlor. And Grandmother came with her cute little butter petal and gave him fifty smacks and one to go on. And they were so afraid I'd give this away because I adored Dad and I wasn't about to let him be punished for having his wonderful birthday. And they were scared, I'd tell him, so they cautioned me and shook their fingers under my nose. I was not to tell him. So when he came in, his brothers grabbed him and had him down in a minute. Lots of fun. Lots of fun. And then the last thing I found out about Grandmother Jane, the Jubilee Singers, these were a traveling Negro group. And they were high class people who sang opera and very lovely music, skilled musicians. They also sang spirituals and delighted everyone with those. But there I got to go to both of those programs. My memory is very long. I'm quite sure I can remember being rocked by my mother when my head was cradled in her left elbow, inside elbow. I am sure I can remember that memory. And I remember being three years old because I stood in the door between the kitchen and the dining room on the ranch. It wasn't a dining room, it was the front room. I stood in that doorway and I could just reach my fingers across from one casing to the other and think I am three years old.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: I remember being put down for naps because of flies. We have no problem with insects here. But, oh, insects were such a problem in pioneer times. Mother was besieged by the awful problem of bedbugs on the farm because part of one room was boarded from the homesteader shack where the wood had bedbugs in it. And my mother didn't have them by the time I gave a long goodness nose, but she had struggled mightily to get rid of those insects because in her good housekeeping standards that was nothing but dirt that you allowed around if you had a bedbug. Well, I saw them in my life because from time to time a hired man or sheep herder bedroll would get left on a porch and in they'd come. Or they'd land in the bunkhouse from the back of the hack or a wagon from a sheep camp or a cattle camp and there would be those creatures and she'd have to go to work again. And, oh, it would just be in her frenzy that she'd go after those. And once I had an experience myself, which might be of interest, at Idaho State, which was then the Southern branch of the University of Idaho, I was a house mother since my husband, who was a football coach, was a professor of physical education. So we lived in this dormitory as Proctor and Hostess. And this weekend we planned to entertain all of the parents of the 58 young men who lived in our dormitory for overnight. Young men were going to sleep on the floor with each other or any other way they could manage, but the parents were to have their double -deggers. And we had everything in absolute apple pie order. When in came a senior pharmacy student from the room across the hall to say, Mrs. Wicks, there are bedbugs in my room. Oh, what an awful thing that was. Well, of course, we sealed it off and the maintenance people came over and as soon as the company was gone, cyanide was used and that took care of that. But of course, all of us had to get out of the building for a while. But the nail of utter despair that he brought to me when he's in their bedbugs in my room since I'd had all this childish conditioning that this is the worst thing that can happen to any housekeeper. That was a dismaying thing. Well, to get back to my long memory, I can remember being laid down on my mother's bed for a rest. So I must have been fairly small and that the flies bothered me. And those days you use sticky fly paper, you use poison fly paper, you use that old fly swatter, which was not commercial, it was home done. Sometimes it was a newspaper, sometimes it slit and sometimes just in a spanker. Sometimes it was a piece of leather, nailed to a little board of hand holder size. It was various things, but oh, how the flies and insects were fought and how much I do appreciate the ease with which we live these days. Well now, to get back to early life and Genesee, I'll go to my own childhood because actually our family retired and dad moved to town when I was four. And we went back to the farm for summers until I was seven and then the big boys took over very soon and then we just went back during World War I. Which brings up something I don't let me get by until I tell you and that's playing for movies which came during that interlude and I may forget to tell you this. But the movies of course were silent and they were black and white and they hired someone who played the piano to make the sound effect. The show was only shown once so one didn't have much practice in what was to be come next, but they did have a libretto and I could read that and when the march was indicated I would have the Plattsburgh march or the Hungarian march done by Litz. Or the General Pershing march or any of the Stars and Stripes forever, any of these marches I would have. Or they would indicate perhaps a lullaby and I would have everything from Brahms to a Negro one. Or they would have a love story and I'd have everything from Love's Old Sweet Song to Till We Meet Again or Marquita or whatever. And so if the man, he paid me two and a half a night and if he'd only known I would have paid him for the prestige and the joy of it. But when it came time to be on the farm in the summer, this presented quite a problem because to get to Saturday night show my dad had to hitch up the horse and take me, but he was determined I was to keep this nice lucrative job. So he'd take me to town and we'd go to the show. I can't remember whether he went or not, he probably didn't, probably went over to the pool hall and played a little game of solo.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: But when the show was over it would be pitch black at night. We had no light of any kind, but that horse would take us home. And if you can imagine how black a night can be, usually there's a little light outside, but some nights are black where you don't have electric lights around anywhere. You'd be amazed at how far electric lights show and how accustomed we are to them now. But I remember those as the blackest trips and of course I'd been under nervous strain to play for this show and while I enjoyed it, it took a lot out of me. And I would be so exhausted by the time we got home, but it was only once a week and we would only be there maybe a few weeks and it would be over. So I wanted to be sure to talk about that before we could eat this. Oh, I could watch the movie entirely. The only thing was I had problems in the instrument. The piano was hoisted from the stage to the floor and the floor to the stage for each event that came in the opera house and of course this didn't help tuning much. And also peanut shells seemed to wander in around and finally the whole center octave would not make a sound and I complained. I could play in the upper octaves in the lower ones, but the middle octave simply made no response to my fingers. And so Mr. Herman had a tuner come. They took a two quart can of peanut shells out from that instrument and a little boy's cap.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: But I got to see that show. I tell you I had the most wonderful time with those good shows. There was never a dud. I never saw a bad show. They were all good to my eyes. Oh, such a lovely evening I would have. And later on when I had bows why it was delicious from start to finish that particular night of the week. Well now to get back to early genesee. In my day there was nothing to do unless you stirred it up yourself in the way of recreation. We had lots of little clubs and we danced and we had lots of little fun games that we played and we were all expert at cards.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Pedro 500, Whist, Hearts, Peanutle and eventually Contract Bridge, but that didn't come till along in the twenties. We could have a happy time with a batch of fudge and a deck of cards and each other. Or another thing we did that was lots of fun. If somebody would play the piano, everybody would barbershop harmonize. And if somebody could play the violin with them, oh that was really boldness. And we would dance and we would have pop popcorn and we would have so much good fun. But the tinkle of the old piano was what was the center of the gaiety. And goodness sakes, I played for funerals, I played for weddings, I played for shows. We had lots of home telling plays, just worlds of them. And eventually I grew up to the place where I had the lead here and there. And I was determined I was never going to be kissed until the man who asked me to marry him kissed me. We were very much along those lines those days. We kept ourselves pretty much to ourselves and we didn't touch everybody. And they accepted dignified dancing and it was very dignified. But I had to kiss a man. He was in the bank. He had ugly teeth. And I was the maid in the play and he was the policeman. And at one point I had to kiss that man. And I bet he didn't get one bit of pleasure out of it because I made myself the most rigid little piece of cardboard you ever saw. I suppose he had many laughs about that to himself, but I was most uncomfortable. Oh dear. Well, let's see, some early genesee. Going to town to trade was an event. It happened once a week, sometimes. And then again it would happen just when it was handy. Or when in bad weather dad could go on horseback or one of the big boys. Horseback meant a limited order. But of course we didn't buy too much out of the grocery store anyway. Mainly coffee, tea, sugar, and salt, vanilla. Things of that kind because we raised about everything else. But dad paid the grocery bill once a year when he sold maybe take a load of potatoes to Lewiston on the big old wagon. And he'd come back with it loaded with everything from colored crayons to new underwear. Oh, it was delicious. Or else he'd come back with a cash and mother daughter from Sears or Monkey Ward. Of course those catalogs were of great use from the time they came until they were used leaf by leaf in the tucket, as my grandmother called it. But we set great store by trading. And being the baby of the family and much indulged, dad used to kid me because I always used to say, bring me a sing and he'd say a nice papa. And I would look crestfallen and then he'd bring out the little striped sack of lemon drops or what I called little bays. They were these little lumpy covered peanuts, but they were bay color so I call them little bays. Or once in a while it'd be striped stick candy. And stick candy was a great joy because what you could do with a stick of candy was suck the end of it till it got as sharp as a needle. And then you could go around pretending that you had a surgeon's needle and you could kind of make a little dance in your skin with it. But of course that left a little sweet drop there and you got a little sticky after a while, but I thought they were great sport. My older brothers and sister were fair game for me to ask for nickels. And one time my brother Ben, I hid up on the main street of Genesee for a little treat. And he didn't have anything smaller than a fifth cent piece, but he gave it to me. And I went into Smolt's Confectionery and I spent it all for Nigger Faces, which was a little licorice candy. And then I fed all of my friends and we ate till we couldn't face them anymore and eventually so that no one would find out how foolish I had been because I can realize that this was completely out of line. I buried them in the cracks of the wooden sidewalk and for years walking over that batch of boards I felt a wave of deep guilt. But my brothers were so good to me and even my brother Donna was only four years older. When there came a stock show one time, I probably was about six and he was ten or seven and eleven. He shined shoes all day and he made ten dollars and he gave me a dollar to spend.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Generous and dear, but that older, down -to -earth brother Lou, what did he do?

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: He was working in the bank then and he gave me ten cents and I could use it on the wheel that spun and brought you delicious prizes. And he said, okay, you can spend it any place you want to, but just once. Don't you ever do it again because those aren't good articles that you get from the wheel. So I spun the wheel just once and that little flapper deal stopped where there was a beautiful vase and the lady wrote Grace on it. And I proudly showed it to him just kind of silently and maybe very indirectly tell him that the little pointer didn't always stop at a bad place with cheap stuff. Look what I had. I suppose he chuckled about that too. But that was quite a thing. Stock show was the big event of the summer. The men took great pride during this interlude of the early teens, oh, 1912, 13, 14, etc. in the fine stock they had on their farms. And they imported good stallions and they had heavy draft horses that were Belgians and they would make much of their stock. This was before Hereford's, but they had good milk cows, Holsteins and Gernsees and jerseys and so forth. And they had good horses. The horses were the main thing. And they had fine pigs. And they would show these at a stock show that lasted about three days in Genesee. And my goodness, Mother would plan on that. And so our whole wardrobe for the summer around the needs of the stock show. It rained, which sometimes it did. And our little white shoes and our embroidered dresses and everything would get soaked. Oh my, that was the worst thing. But we'd be out on the farm, all moved in. And when we came to town, there'd be no furniture there, but just the running gears, Dad would call it, of how we could batch it during the celebration. And Mother would have a boiled ham already and she'd have lots of bread ready and her delicious butter and jelly. And we'd bring milk. And we'd batch for a few days. And of course my grandmother always lived in town. And her house was always open to us with delicious food and hospitality. We also had an uncle who had the barber shop down. And his home was mighty good pickings too. And so we didn't have any place that we ate except at the family table. Because we wouldn't dream of buying a hamburger from the little quick and greasy food selling stand because we were sure it wasn't clean. We were just positive that that precise the flies were around. And no doubt saw that meat did get polluted. But we didn't have to depend on that because we could always go and have proper food. But it would be a three day celebration and it was greatly looked forward to. Our fun would be augmented by a merry -go -round that would come at the Muts and O 'Hare's wheel. And the music from either one of them was such that to this day I think that circuses have lost out. And many times shows of this kind have lost out because they don't have the music anymore. I noticed at the fair this time they had music again but sometimes it's a silent celebration. And that just doesn't lift people's spirits at all. When the circuses came and oh what an event they were. And went parading down the street with the calliope blaring forth in some delicious series of sounds. And they have to be especially in a tune. Just tooting. Oh my goodness you could hardly wait to ask your dad for the money to go to that circus. Now that there isn't an in it's silent and off by itself on the edge of time. Who cares whether you go or not. It just doesn't whip you up at all. And it seemed to me that the decline of circus attendance accompanied the lack of music. I've always thought this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of music did they have then?

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Sometimes with larger circuses they would have a little band. And the Roused Bouts or whoever doubled for Trappies or whatever would tutor on when he had an idle moment. And there would always be a big bass drum. And whatever there was to carry the melody would be pressed into service. And the uniform would consist of any kind of hands. But a match matching coats and hats. Usually with a stockade on a little decoration on the hat. And then when we would see these same people in work clothes gathering up the tent or moving the animal cages or whatever. We'd be so surprised because he'd been so grand a few minutes before in fine clothes in the ring perhaps whipping horses through their act or taking a chair in a blank cartridge or two to the lion's act or whatever you know. We didn't have very many big circuses in Genesee but we had them in Moscow and we had them in Lewiston. And going to Lewiston was a tremendous experience because that's where you got your new shoes. That's where when you needed glasses you finally got your glasses. That's where you drank your first milkshake. That's where you had the lovely experience of going to your first dance with a bow. Oh my. Not first dance with a bow as such really because my first dance was at the Knights of Pythias Lodge when my father and my three brothers were pressed into service to lead my faltering steps around the floor. I think my first dance was when I was about 13 which would be in 1919. And I know my big brothers were just home from the war and they weren't much for dancing. It wasn't their cup of tea but my sister loved to and my younger brother did and I did. And the reason those older boys didn't was because their hearts loves didn't care much about dancing. So they kind of were lost as partners along the way but by going to Lewiston to my first dance that meant getting into a man's car and riding and maybe being taken to dinner and then up to the dream land where they had a multi - A ball with little mirrors all around it and those little mirrors would flash lights in most dazzling array around the walls of the building and the man brother's orchestra was well known in this area. And one of them was a redhead as I recall and goodness they made marvelous music and that was a date to be paid attention to. Of course I might have long hair down my back and highly shoes and not be with it like the girls who had bobbed hair and rolled socks and high heevils. But finally I grew up and got those things too.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Dancing in your own family's loving circle is a pretty nice way for a little girl to learn. This is what the Mormons have done for generations and it's a real nice experience for the whole family. I went to a Mormon dance down in Soda Springs, Idaho one time which was for the benefit of a missionary going to convert the heathen in Switzerland which didn't seem very realistic to me but that's where he was going and of course he was taking the Mormon faith there which was not their faith. And I danced all evening with people named Lau, L -A -U, who were pioneer Mormon proselytizers that had been sent by Brigham Young up there to settle along with some other families. And I danced with a boy seventeen and I danced with a man seventy -two. I had no partners but Laus and my husband and I forget how much money, a hundred and fifty -four or something like that dollars they gleaned for the young man that night and of course it was a family affair. As sweetly done and as wholesomely done and as happily done as any event you can figure up to do.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: I wander away from Genesee pretty badly don't I?

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: Let me tell some more about some of the stores because this trading business was lots of fun. One of the stores that I have mentioned was Smolt's Confectionery. Mr. Smolt was a cigar maker who did his cigars by hand from imported leaves shipped in from Cuba and Havana. And his delightful very much the lady wife who was a gazelle chin was around in the business all of their lives. This was a lovely place for children to go or anybody to go. They had the nice tables and chairs with the wrought twisted backs and legs and the marble circles for the tables. They had lovely silver holders for the glasses for ice cream sodas and lovely containers for Sundays with the proper length of handle on the spoons. And their products were of excellent quality. Mr. Smolt also was a practiced candy maker and he made his own chocolate creams. And we had a delightful experience in our Confectionery store. And Ed Smolt the son carried on and as long as this happened Smolt's was a center of delight for young people. And chess might be being played on a table somewhere with Ed and the dentist or the veterinarian or somebody. It was also a drama center because Ed loved to play the lead in our home talent productions. And he loved to wear the costumes that were rented and brought back from Spokane. And he was the center of much gaiety and pleasure in the community. Then the next place was the post office which was always run by somebody who was a friend whether it was Democrat appointee or Republican appointee depending on who the president's political party was. Then up the street a little ways there was a Jake Grossenstein store. He was an early day Jew who came to settle very early. Probably one of the first merchants and he moved his store into the location I speak of as Genesee because the first Genesee was out at the four corners on the flat which I can point out to you. But when the railroad put its depot a mile, a quarter I guess it was, west of where the community was, why the community had to move to the depot. And the railroad tracks, someone had tried to hold up the Northern Pacific people in the cost of getting right of way so they just balked and didn't do it. And so Mr. Grossenstein moved to his store and his store always had a peculiar odor. It was a pickled herring of dry goods, leather, cheese and unaired closeness. And there weren't any windows except in the front which meant that it was a dark prospect that one went into with this odor. And I some way associated this with the Hebrew race for years. But they were lovely people. And they gave my grandparents on their golden wedding anniversary a silver service of which the sugar and creamer I still use.

speaker: Grace Wicks
words: And another store across the...
